episode_title: Ole Reissmann: Revolutionizing a Legacy Newsroom with AI at Germany's Der Spiegel

codes.description: Framing AI adoption decisions around whether use cases serve organizational mission and values rather than chasing the most obvious or hyped applications
excerpts: Maybe the most obvious use cases of generative AI are not the ones we should go after. Because like first we have to ask ourselves why we would want to use AI and what AI is actually good at. And does it serve our mission and does it align with our values?
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: The tendency for users to treat LLMs as factual search engines due to familiar chat-box interfaces, despite LLMs generating language rather than retrieving verified knowledge
excerpts: many of these AI tools have presented themselves to us just like a search engine. And yes, also as a chat interface but as a search engine and then in small print at the bottom it's like well maybe after all maybe this is nonsense maybe you should check it
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: LLMs producing factual outputs that are partially correct but fundamentally wrong, illustrated through a personal anecdote of querying an LLM about oneself
excerpts: I asked about myself and it gave I mean, it tried to give facts and the facts it spit out was it was just it was not completely wrong, but at the same time completely wrong. So it put me in the vicinity of like he's a journalist, he studied politics Which true, but then and history, which false, at a university where I did not study
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: The recognition that providing LLMs with context and grounding data (RAG approach) dramatically improves output quality compared to relying on parametric knowledge alone
excerpts: if you just make the prompt bigger, if you give the machine context, if you give the machine more data, then it gets exciting
I do not trust the LLM and the probability model... I'm gonna put some authoritative sources in there and ask about what did the experts actually say
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.code: SEO automation as entry-point use case
codes.description: Using AI to automate routine SEO headline generation as a low-risk, high-volume first deployment that frees editorial staff for higher-value work
excerpts: our SEO department was asking, well, we are checking a hundred fifty articles on any given day for a good SEO headline. Isn't there something AI can do for us? And of course there is something you can do with AI. So in that case we helped our SEO department and our SEO editors free up their time to do something more useful
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: AI initiatives emerging from departments themselves (SEO, fact-checking) rather than being pushed top-down by the AI team, indicating organic demand
excerpts: it's not us that's pushing it, not the AI team, but our fact checking department was thinking to themselves like maybe we can do a first round of fact checking an article
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Using AI to extract factual claims from articles and verify them via web search agents and databases, handling routine verification so human fact-checkers can focus on complex cases
excerpts: We run the fact checking tool not to get rid of the fact checking department, no no, to the contrary

it only gets you like I don't know twenty, thirty, forty percent there. And the other percent, that's like the stuff humans are really needed for and which is really complicated and where you can't do a web search, like where people have to really dig into it
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Assigning probability scores to factual claims based on verification source quality, enabling fact-checkers to triage their effort
excerpts: We're giving a score. Like we can do a score how sure is the model that this fact may be actually true
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Investing in organization-wide AI literacy with structured training programs that consistently sell out, reaching 870+ staff across departments
excerpts: more than 870 people have taken part in these basic training sessions and when we announce them they are booked out, so we are offering more and more of them
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Training the entire organization so AI policy decisions emerge from broad participation rather than top-down mandates
excerpts: we want to educate as many people as possible to be able to be educated and take part in these discussions. So that these decisions do not come necessarily only from the top, but like the whole company talks about this stuff
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Favoring flexible guiding principles over strict rules for AI use, given the rapidly shifting technological landscape and user expectations
excerpts: I don't think like we are setting strict guidelines now because this stuff is changing all the time. So maybe we have some principles now and then we like have discussions along the way
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: An explicit editorial red line against using AI-generated photorealistic images in journalism to prevent fabricating visual truth
excerpts: we do not want to use photorealistic images because we do not want to make up the truth and we don't want to trick anybody
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: External software tools continuously adding AI features (image manipulation buttons) that may cross editorial lines, creating an uncontrollable compliance challenge
excerpts: any other day, a software will have a new button, a new magic button that does something. And maybe you are maybe even you got tricked into using that button, and then this button does something to an image... But I mean we can't control these software tools, right?
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: A legacy newsroom prioritizing audience trust above speed-to-market, deliberately delaying consumer-facing AI products to avoid mistakes
excerpts: We are really in the trust business and if we were to put something out, we want to make sure that not only a younger audience is happy, but our core audience, which tends to be a little bit older, our paying subscribers, we want to offer them factual information and we really want to make sure that we do not disappoint our readers and that we do not lose trust
maybe we're not the first ones doing it. Maybe we don't have to be the first ones doing it. Better do this one right
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Concentrating initial AI tool development on internal-facing applications (10 projects, only 1-2 audience-facing) to build competence before external exposure
excerpts: we have like 10 projects going on and there's only one or two that are like outside facing
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Using the newsroom's own article archive as an authoritative source for RAG-based AI tools, grounding outputs in verified journalism rather than LLM parametric knowledge
excerpts: we ask ChatGPT to look into our archives, read some articles, summarize the information of these articles, and get out a paragraph with linked sources
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Requiring human review and approval of AI-generated article summaries before publication, even at the cost of additional labor
excerpts: summaries which are yes generated by ChatGPT but then checked by a human. The human checks the summary, decides, well this is good, good enough. If it's good enough, he's gonna correct it and makes it really good and then decides to publish it
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: A proof-of-concept system that ingests breaking news articles, generates audio-optimized scripts, converts to speech, and translates into multiple languages with human checkpoints
excerpts: we've been building a machine that ingests spiegel articles. And then you apply a business logic... take the article, make a audio version out of it which is more written for audio, shorter sentences... And maybe even translate it into Russian and English and Turkish
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Building prototypes to demonstrate what is technically possible while explicitly withholding production commitment until user testing validates need
excerpts: this is a proof of concept. I'm not sure this will see the light of the day, but we want to stay on top of the technological advancement and show what's possible out there. Because it's certainly possible to do these things, but we want to ask ourselves is this something the world needs right now
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Treating the vector database of the article archive as the single most important AI project because it enables multiple downstream applications (personalization, search, RAG)
excerpts: Our most important project... is this personalization. We really want to do it... to be able to do that, we will have a vector database with our articles. Once we have this database, we can build upon it, and there are so many other things we can imagine afterwards
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Using LLMs to extract names and infer gender from articles, producing departmental reports that reveal systematic gender imbalance in coverage -- turning AI's pattern-detection against editorial blind spots
excerpts: There's so much talk about, oh well, these AI LLMs, they are biased. And now it's actually an LLM that's helping us discover our biases. I think that's quite beautiful
on average, there are many more men that are appearing in our articles. And now we... looking into this data and talking to departments
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Accepting imperfect AI-generated metrics (gender identification not 100% accurate) because the directional signal is still vastly more useful than having no data
excerpts: it's not gonna be a hundred percent correct and I'm very sorry, but it gives you a tendency. And if the numbers are as our numbers are, we are really looking at numbers that we are really comfortable in taking these numbers for granted
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Growing awareness that AI companies previously trained on publisher content without consent, prompting new vigilance about data protection and European data residency
excerpts: in the past all the companies trained on our data. And we weren't like so aware or so acutely aware that this might be a problem. So I think there is a new sense of well, maybe let's protect our data
with Microsoft Azure they tell you you can choose to let the data stay in Europe which is for some people very important
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Building a custom internal platform connecting to enterprise AI APIs (Azure, OpenAI, Google) with data protection guarantees, ensuring staff can experiment safely
excerpts: We build our own. It's just like we have enterprise accounts with Microsoft Azure, with ChatGPT, with Google. We will make sure that they do not train on the data
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Younger audiences and students displaying notably greater comfort with AI-generated content (cloned voices, avatars) compared to older professionals, creating a tension in editorial standards
excerpts: younger audiences Sometimes you have students with projects they are working on... I think they're sometimes much more comfortable with AI tools, AI usage, cloned voices, avatars where a person my age is more reluctantly like, oh well yes, it's possible, but should we really make use of it?
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Factoring compute energy costs and environmental sustainability into AI product decisions, not just financial costs
excerpts: this compute, it doesn't come from nowhere, it costs energy, and is this a good use of energy right now
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Framing AI-powered personalization as expanding reader autonomy in choosing content format (read, listen, summarize) rather than just algorithmic recommendation
excerpts: this new technology makes more things possible that weren't possible before in terms of personalization and I as a user have a higher degree of autonomy in choosing what piece of content do I want to consume? In what format?
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Concern that if news organizations do not offer AI-powered features themselves, browsers, operating systems, or new platforms will provide these functions and disintermediate publishers
excerpts: do we want to cede some of these functions to browsers or operating systems or new platforms? Or do we want to offer some of these new technology ourselves?
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: The hypothesis that LLM-powered voice interfaces may revive the failed smart speaker use case from four years ago now that machines understand natural language far better
excerpts: maybe we will have a second coming of the smart speaker and suddenly these machines understand us much better and maybe people are much more likely to talk to these machines... that's like ninety percent more than before
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: German publishers (Der Spiegel, Axel Springer) sharing costs and resources on a joint press archive and AI infrastructure, recognizing that even major publishers are effectively small tech companies
excerpts: we are one of the major publishing houses in Germany. Still, we are a small, mid-sized company. So doing tech is hard for us. And for the others as well. And I think collaborating on projects, sharing knowledge, it's very important because otherwise we couldn't keep up
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.code: national press archive with AI layer
codes.description: A shared database of every article published in Germany since the Federal Republic, now exploring generative AI chat interfaces for research access
excerpts: we are running a database which basically includes every article ever published since the beginning of the Federal Republic. And now we are looking how can we make use of Generative AI to access all the articles in a better way
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Conceptualizing AI's role as transforming existing journalistic content (SEO headline, translation, summary, audio) rather than creating original journalism
excerpts: after there is an article, what can we do? To transform the article, an SEO headline is a transformation, a translation is a transformation, a summary is a transformation
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Envisioning AI as a tool to help journalists access and analyze large volumes of unstructured data to produce investigative insights
excerpts: we really want to teach people and help people with investigations and get to know tools, have ideas, how to make use of generative AI, not to write articles, but to access huge amounts of data, unstructured data, what have you, apply AI mechanisms... and producing insights, information and building articles upon that
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Newer, better-sounding text-to-speech models designed for short-form content struggling with Der Spiegel's long-form articles (40+ minutes), requiring workarounds
excerpts: most of these newer models are actually built for I don't know for speech assistance and so on and so forth and for shorter forms of content. So if we have a really long article which is 40 minutes or more. You have to come up with some workarounds
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Anecdotal experience of uploading personal blood work to ChatGPT and receiving an assessment that matched a doctor's evaluation, illustrating cross-domain AI applicability
excerpts: I got my blood work done... I uploaded a picture and sent it to ChatGPT... and I got a comprehensive assessment with explanations. And when I catch up with my doctor later, she told me basically the same things
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

codes.description: Using Google NotebookLM to upload podcast transcripts and query them for expert perspectives on a project proposal, demonstrating RAG as a method for synthesizing existing knowledge
excerpts: I applied for this AI journalism lab at CUNY... my thinking was if I apply with a project, I could ask what you and all the other fabulous guests of the podcast would think about my proposal. So I did some transcripts... you upload the text, it gets vectorized into the embeddings into a database, and then you ask questions
codes.speaker: Ole Reissmann

episode_summary: This episode reveals a legacy newsroom navigating AI adoption through a strategy of deliberate caution anchored in institutional values rather than technological novelty. Ole Reissmann articulates a distinctive approach where AI's role is framed primarily as content transformation and workflow augmentation rather than original content creation, with the most ambitious projects (vector database, personalization) positioned as foundational infrastructure. The tension between competitive pressure to deploy consumer-facing AI products and the imperative to preserve subscriber trust emerges as the central strategic dilemma, resolved through an internal-first deployment philosophy. Notably, Der Spiegel's use of AI to audit its own gender bias in coverage represents a counter-narrative to the dominant framing of AI-as-bias-amplifier, instead positioning the technology as a mirror for editorial self-reflection. The episode also surfaces an underexplored theme of inter-publisher collaboration driven by the recognition that even major European newsrooms are functionally small tech companies.
surprises: Der Spiegel's most exciting AI project is not a generative content tool but a gender bias audit that uses LLMs to surface systemic underrepresentation of women in coverage -- turning the 'biased AI' narrative on its head.
The fact-checking tool is explicitly designed to only get 20-40% of the work done, with the remaining complexity reserved for humans -- a refreshingly honest framing of AI limitations in production.

Over 870 staff at a single German newsroom have completed basic AI training, with sessions consistently selling out -- suggesting demand for AI literacy far exceeds supply even in well-resourced organizations.

Ole personally uploaded his blood work to ChatGPT and received medically accurate advice, demonstrating a pragmatic personal relationship with AI that contrasts with his professional caution about consumer-facing products.
tensions: Between being first-to-market with AI features and preserving the legacy brand trust that subscribers pay for -- explicitly resolved in favor of caution but creating competitive vulnerability.
Between younger audiences who are comfortable with AI-generated content (cloned voices, avatars) and the older paying subscriber base that expects traditional journalistic standards.
Between the aspiration for comprehensive AI literacy across 870+ staff and the reality that technology changes faster than training programs can update.
Between the desire to protect publisher data from AI training and the historical reality that publishers already gave away their content to platform companies for years without awareness.
Between building AI tools in-house and the resource constraints of being a 'small, mid-sized company' despite being one of Germany's largest newsrooms.
